g.text: This means the worker will need to acquire a lock before updating the row
Partition messages by charityId to enable batching
and preserve order for per-charity processing.
Avoid donationIntentId as a key, it's high-cardinality
and limits batching efficiency or implement a custom
partitioner to spread load for popular charities.
If charityId becomes a hot key, we will use a
The none and timestamp will serve as
Build alarms or move to DLQ for repeated failures
status = INITIATED or PENDING older than X minutes (e.g., 15 mins)
If still pending, logs and reschedules
If failed, updates Transaction.status and emits donation.failed
If retriable error (e.g., network timeout), retries max 3 times with exponential backoff
If Stripe failure was due to idempotency race or ambiguity, flags for human/manual review
We use optimistic locking via a version column on the Charity row
1) Aggregation worker reads the current totalAmount and version (e.g., v1)
3) Attempts UPDATE ... WHERE id = $id AND version = v1
4) If 0 rows affected, someone else already updated → retry
Why we prefer optimistic over pessimistic:
3) Avoids holding locks for the entire update duration
What Happens if two donations come in the same second?
This is exactly where conflict handling kicks in.
- With optimistic locking in aggregation worker, one update will succeed, and the other will retry with the new version.
- Kafka ensures per-charity message ordering within a partition if partitioned by charityId, which helps serialize those updates.
- To further reduce collision probability, we batch updates — the Aggregation Worker can prefetch and group all Kafka messages
per charity every 1–2 seconds before updating.
If the system evolves for even larger scale, we can look into CRDTs to avoid conflicts here.
That will help gain latency and availability on a global level, but lose some consistency.
The system is designed around event-driven, idempotent donation intents
that flow through Kafka to asynchronous workers, enabling reliable, scalable,
and eventually consistent updates to donation aggregates without blocking the user or payment flow
1) Immediate failures (sync - card declined, insufficient funds etc)
- Stripe API returns an error immediately on transaction creation
Notification Worker: informs donor of failure
Reconciliation Worker: optionally retries payment or flags for support
2) Delayed failures (async failures - web hook based, 3DS auth timeout, fraud detection, expired payment)
- Stripe sends a webhook with failed status (e.g., payment_intent.payment_failed)
Aggregation worker ignores this event (no amount added)
Users can donate to a listed charity via a secure payment flow
Users can view a list of charities and filter by category or keyword
Users can view their own donation history (optional anonymous flow supported)
System tracks and displays total donations per charity
Security: All payment flows are PCI-compliant; no sensitive card data is stored
Reliability: System handles retries, webhooks, and failures using idempotency and reconciliation
Performance: Supports 10–20 transactions per second with fast response times
Availability > Consistency: Donations should not fail even if aggregates are eventually updated
Do we need to account for any donation campaigns with start and end times?
We could even store web hook events as a separate core entity if needed
GET /users/me/donation ---> 200, donations[] --- we can expand this to reflect for
more users than just "me" if we are building an admin tool
Only process if the key doesn't exist
